planting.note: Sow seeds direct in early spring in deep, sandy to gravelly, well-drained, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; sea kale requires 2–3 years of establishment before harvesting — patience is rewarded with long-lived, extremely productive plants.

directions: Sow seeds direct in early spring in deep, sandy to gravelly, well-drained, neutral to alkaline soil in full sun; sea kale has a very long taproot and deeply resents transplanting — choose its permanent location carefully. Crambe maritima is a spectacular, ornamental-as-well-as-edible native of European coastal shingle beaches, producing large, blue-gray, crinkled, cabbage-like leaves and billowing clouds of tiny, honey-scented white flowers on 24–36-inch stems in early summer that are outstanding in the border; the young, blanched shoots are the edible crop, harvested in early spring by covering the dormant crown with a forcing pot or dark bucket 4–6 weeks before harvest, producing pale, sweetly nutty, asparagus-like shoots. Established plants are extremely drought-tolerant, salt-tolerant, and long-lived, thriving in poor, well-drained soils in coastal and hot, dry inland gardens.

planting.note: Sow direct after all danger of frost in warm, well-drained soil in full sun; soak seeds overnight to improve germination — New Zealand spinach thrives in summer heat where true spinach bolts.

directions: Sow direct after all danger of frost has passed in warm, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; soak seeds 12–24 hours in warm water before sowing to soften the hard seed coat and improve germination — thin to 18–24 inches apart. New Zealand spinach is the premier heat-tolerant, summer-substitute for true spinach, producing a sprawling, succulent-leaved groundcover of succulent, triangular, bright-green leaves with a mild, slightly salty flavor that can be harvested continuously by tip-pruning throughout summer and fall without bolting; it is ideal for the Gulf Coast and similar hot, humid climates where true spinach is impossible in summer. Despite the common name it is not closely related to spinach; it is in the family Aizoaceae. Frost-tender but can naturalize in zone 9–10 from self-seeding.

planting.note: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in any reasonably fertile, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; orache germinates in cool soil and is one of the fastest-growing cool-season greens.

directions: Sow direct 4–6 weeks before last frost in any reasonably fertile, well-drained to moist, neutral to slightly alkaline soil in full sun to partial shade; orache germinates and grows extremely rapidly in cool conditions and can be harvested as baby leaf greens in 3 weeks or as full plants at 40–60 days. Atriplex hortensis is a large, fast-growing cool-season annual producing striking red, gold, or green arrow-shaped to triangular leaves with a mild, slightly salty, spinach-like flavor excellent for salads, stir-fries, and as a spinach substitute; the red form ('Rubra') is especially ornamental, growing to 3–6 feet tall and making a dramatic statement in the potager. It self-seeds prolifically and can naturalize in gardens; in the Gulf South it is best grown in fall, winter, and spring as a cool-season crop. Related to quinoa and lamb's quarters; all parts including the young seed heads are edible.

planting.note: Sow direct in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; garden sorrel is extremely easy to grow and will produce harvests within 2 months of sowing.

directions: Sow direct in early spring 4–6 weeks before last frost in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; garden sorrel is one of the easiest and most productive perennial vegetables — once established, remove flowering stalks to maintain leaf production and divide clumps every 3–4 years to rejuvenate. Rumex acetosa is a European native perennial herb grown for its large, arrow-shaped leaves with a distinctive, bright, lemony-sour flavor from oxalic acid that is the defining ingredient in classic French sorrel soup, sorrel sauce (excellent with fish), and sorrel-cream pasta; the leaves can be used fresh in salads when young and small, or cooked and pureed when larger, though they turn an unappetizing khaki color on cooking. The plant is extremely cold-hardy, re-emerging reliably in early spring, and is one of the first fresh greens available in the spring garden; harvest leaves continuously from the outside of the clump. Consume in moderation due to oxalic acid content.

planting.note: Sow direct or plant divisions in early spring in well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; French sorrel is lower and more sprawling than garden sorrel with smaller, more arrowhead-shaped leaves and a more refined, less harsh acid flavor.

directions: Sow direct or plant divisions in early spring in well-drained, sandy to loamy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; French sorrel is more drought-tolerant than garden sorrel and thrives in poor, well-drained soils — rich, moist soil produces rank growth with coarser flavor. Rumex scutatus is a low-growing, somewhat sprawling European perennial herb with small, distinctive, shield-shaped or hastate (arrowhead-shaped) leaves that have a milder, more refined, less harsh acid flavor than the common garden sorrel (R. acetosa) and are preferred by many chefs for their superior taste — it is the traditional sorrel used in fine French cuisine and is sometimes called "true French sorrel." It is an excellent perennial groundcover in the kitchen garden, spilling attractively over path edges and walls; unlike R. acetosa it tolerates dry, rocky conditions. Harvest the small leaves continuously and remove flower stalks to prevent self-seeding.

planting.note: Direct sow in fall (or spring in cold climates) in moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun to partial shade; miner's lettuce self-seeds freely and will often naturalize in cool, moist, shaded areas.

directions: Direct sow in fall (in mild climates zones 7–10) or early spring in cool, moist, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade; thin to 4–6 inches apart or simply broadcast and allow to self-thin — miner's lettuce grows rapidly in cool weather and is often harvested as a baby leaf green 3–4 weeks after sowing. Claytonia perfoliata is a western North American native cool-season annual producing distinctive disc-shaped leaves that appear to be perforated by the flower stalk — a unique and charming botanical feature; both the young cotyledon leaves and the mature, cup-shaped perfoliate leaves are edible with a mild, crisp, slightly succulent texture and flavor similar to very mild spinach or watercress, excellent in salads and as a garnish. It was used by California Gold Rush miners as a vitamin C-rich green in winter and spring (giving rise to the common name), grows in sun or shade, and self-seeds prolifically to naturalize in moist, shaded garden areas in zones 7–10 where it can provide a near-perennial supply of greens.
